The symptoms you're describing - the runny nose with clear discharge, nasal congestion, sore throat, mild headache, drowsiness, and muscle aches - these are all very typical for a viral cold.

The good news is that these infections are usually self-limiting, meaning they get better on their own. They typically last anywhere from 7 to 10 days, and it sounds like you're already about five days in.

What I'd recommend is to continue with supportive care: make sure you're drinking plenty of fluids, getting good rest, and you can continue using over-the-counter cold medications as needed for symptom relief. Tylenol or Advil can help with the headache and any discomfort. You can also try saline nasal rinses or sprays to help with the congestion.

Yeah, absolutely, I completely understand how frustrating this must be for you, especially having dealt with it for so long. So based on what you've told me today, it really sounds like you're experiencing allergic rhinitis, which is basically inflammation in your nose caused by allergens. The fact that your symptoms get worse in the spring and are triggered by things like dust, and that they've been going on for years in this pattern, really points to this diagnosis. Also, the fact that Reactin helped you is a good sign that this is allergy-related.

So here's what I'd like to do for you. First, I'm going to prescribe you an antihistamine - you mentioned Reactin worked well for you, so we can continue with that or try something similar like Claritin or Aerius that you can take daily, especially during allergy season. I'd also like you to try a nasal steroid spray like Flonase or Nasonex, which can really help with the nasal congestion and the post-nasal drip that's causing your morning sore throat and cough.

I'd also really recommend that you see an allergist. They can do proper allergy testing to figure out exactly what you're allergic to, and they can discuss other treatment options with you, including possibly immunotherapy if needed.

In the meantime, try to avoid your triggers as much as possible - maybe wear a mask when vacuuming, keep windows closed during high pollen days, and consider getting an air purifier for your room.

Does that sound like a good plan to you? Do you have any questions about the medications or anything else?

This is a serious condition and we need to evaluate you right away. I'm going to need to do a physical examination, and we'll need to get some tests done urgently, including blood work - particularly something called a D-dimer - and imaging studies like an ultrasound of your leg and possibly a CT scan of your chest to look for blood clots.

This is actually a known complication after orthopedic surgery, especially knee replacements, which is why it's so important we assess this quickly.

response:
I understand your concern, especially given the current situation with COVID-19. Based on what you've told me - the sore throat, fever, loss of taste, congestion, fatigue, and body aches that started pretty suddenly - COVID-19 is definitely on our list of possibilities. However, these symptoms could also be consistent with a regular upper respiratory infection or cold, especially since you mentioned you typically get a cold once a year in the fall.

What we're going to do is perform a COVID-19 test today to rule that out. We'll do a nasal swab, and depending on which test we use, we should have results within a few hours to a couple of days. In the meantime, I want you to isolate at home - stay away from your family members as much as possible, wear a mask if you need to be around them, and monitor your symptoms closely.

If you develop any worsening symptoms, particularly shortness of breath, chest pain, confusion, or if you have difficulty breathing, you need to seek immediate medical attention or call 911.

For now, you can continue taking Advil for your discomfort and fever, make sure you're staying well hydrated, and get plenty of rest.

Doctor: Okay, thank you for that information. So just to summarize, your one-year-old daughter has had a fever, runny nose, and cough for the past three to four days. The fever has now resolved, but she's developed new wheezing today. The cough was initially productive with clear phlegm but is now dry. She's been fussy but is still eating, drinking, and having adequate wet diapers, which is reassuring. You also noticed some throat redness, and she had a similar episode requiring hospitalization when she was a few months old.

Based on what you've told me, this sounds like it could be bronchiolitis, which is a viral infection that's quite common in young children, especially under two years of age. The progression from upper respiratory symptoms to wheezing is typical. The fact that she had a similar episode before and has a family history of asthma is also important to note.

I'd like to examine her now - I'll listen to her chest, check her breathing rate and effort, her oxygen levels, and do a full examination. Depending on what I find, we may need to do some monitoring, possibly give her some oxygen or breathing treatments if needed, and make sure she stays well hydrated.

Um, in terms of managing your symptoms, you mentioned you've tried antihistamines. I would also recommend maybe trying a decongestant, something like pseudoephedrine or phenylephrine, which might help more with the nasal congestion and runny nose. You can also try some saline nasal rinses or a nasal spray, which can be really helpful for clearing out the nasal passages. Make sure you're staying well hydrated, drinking plenty of fluids, and getting adequate rest.

Um, if your symptoms worsen - if you develop a fever, shortness of breath, chest pain, or any new concerning symptoms - please don't hesitate to come back or call us right away. And once we get your test results back, we'll give you a call to let you know the next steps. But based on what you're telling me today, with no fever, no cough, no loss of taste or smell, and the gradual onset, this could very well just be a viral upper respiratory infection or even a flare of your seasonal allergies, but we want to rule out Covid to be safe.
